options: A rollback of the global ACID transaction across all services
Compensating transactions that semantically undo each prior step
A two-phase commit coordinated at the final commit point
A database savepoint the engine rewinds to on failure
why: A Saga is a sequence of local transactions; on failure it runs compensating transactions to semantically undo prior steps. It trades isolation for availability and eventual consistency.
more: There is no single global transaction to roll back; each service already committed its local work, so the only way back is a forward-running compensation per completed step.

options: A central orchestrator service explicitly drives every step in order
Services react to each other's events via pub/sub with no central coordinator
Services run two-phase commit between each other to stay consistent
A single monolithic service performs every step internally
why: Choreography is decentralized and event-driven: each service listens and reacts, with no central brain. Orchestration is the opposite, where a central coordinator explicitly drives the steps.
more: Easy to reverse, so anchor it: choreography = events fan out, orchestration = one conductor calls each step.

options: Semantic locks that mark records as in-progress until the saga completes
A transactional outbox that publishes events atomically with the local commit
Idempotent compensations so re-running an undo step is safe
A pivot transaction placed first so later steps need no compensation
why: Semantic locks, transactional outbox, and idempotent compensations recover the isolation a Saga gives up. The pivot transaction is the go/no-go step and is placed at the boundary, not first, and does not by itself eliminate compensation for steps before it.
more: A real pivot transaction is a legitimate Saga concept, but it is the point after which the saga can only roll forward; it is not a robustness countermeasure that removes the need to compensate earlier steps, so it is the wrong pick here.

options: A full-duplex bidirectional channel over a single TCP connection
A one-way server-to-client stream over one long-lived HTTP connection with auto-reconnect
A client-to-server-only upload stream over chunked HTTP
A repeated client polling loop that simulates pushing
why: SSE is one-way server-to-client over one persistent HTTP connection, with built-in auto-reconnect. Great for notifications and feeds, but not for bidirectional chat.
more: SSE is text-only (UTF-8) and rides on plain HTTP, so it passes through proxies easily, unlike WebSocket's upgrade handshake.

options: Short polling on a fast interval to approximate real time
Server-Sent Events with a side channel for client messages
WebSocket, giving full-duplex messaging over one connection
Long polling that reopens after each server response
why: WebSocket is full-duplex over a single persistent connection, which is right for chat and games. The cost is that it is stateful and harder to scale than stateless HTTP.
more: SSE plus a POST channel can fake two-way, but it adds latency and complexity versus a true bidirectional socket.

options: Short polling, since each fresh request gets the newest data
Long polling, since it holds requests open until data arrives
SSE, since it streams events over one held-open connection
Long polling and SSE are effectively equal for one-way push
why: Short polling wastes requests, long polling is better, and SSE holds one connection and streams events, making it the most efficient of the three for one-way push.
more: Long polling still incurs a full request/response cycle per message; SSE reuses one connection for many events, so per-message overhead is far lower.

options: Metrics, the aggregate numeric time-series of system health
Logs, the timestamped record of discrete events
Traces, the end-to-end path of a request across services
Backups, the periodic snapshots used for disaster recovery
why: The three pillars are metrics, logs, and traces. Backups are a durability and disaster-recovery concern, not an observability pillar.
more: Layering SLO/SLI and error-budget vocabulary on top of the three pillars signals operational maturity.

options: A reserve fund the team draws on to pay for incident response
The allowed amount of unreliability, equal to 100 percent minus the SLO
The headcount of on-call engineers budgeted per rotation
A circuit-breaker threshold that trips after too many errors
why: The error budget is one minus the SLO. It quantifies acceptable failure and governs the release-versus-risk trade-off, which is a strong operational signal.
more: When the budget is spent, teams typically freeze risky feature launches and redirect effort to reliability until the budget recovers.

options: A circuit breaker that trips open to stop calling a failing dependency
Backpressure that slows or rejects intake when downstream is saturated
Graceful degradation that serves reduced functionality under load
Removing client and server timeouts so requests are never dropped
why: Circuit breakers, backpressure, and graceful degradation all contain cascading failure. Removing timeouts is the opposite: it lets slow calls pile up and makes cascades worse.
more: Timeouts are what let a caller give up on a stuck dependency and free resources; without them, thread pools and connections exhaust system-wide.

options: Does this component add measurable latency to the request path
If this component dies, does the entire system stop serving
Does this component cost more than its redundant alternative
Does this component sit in front of or behind the cache
why: Walk every component and ask whether the system stops if it dies. Eliminate SPOFs via redundancy: multiple instances, replicas, multi-AZ, and auto-failover with no un-backed shared resource.
more: Latency, cost, and caching are real design concerns but none of them define a single point of failure.

options: Multiple stateless instances spread behind a load balancer
Multi-AZ replicas with automatic failover on primary loss
A single shared NFS mount that every node depends on
Active-active deployment across multiple regions
why: Redundancy at every layer removes SPOFs. A single shared NFS mount that everything needs is itself a SPOF, not a fix, since its failure takes down all dependents.
more: The mount could be made redundant (replicated/clustered storage), but as described, one mount everything depends on is the textbook SPOF.

options: About 1,150 QPS on average across the day
About 11,500 QPS on average across the day
About 115,000 QPS on average across the day
About 1,150,000 QPS on average across the day
why: 100M users times 10 requests is 1e9 requests per day. A day is about 86,400 seconds, roughly 1e5, so 1e9 divided by 1e5 is about 10,000, giving roughly 11.5k average QPS.
more: Multiply by about 2 to 3 for peak traffic above the daily average.

options: About 3,600 seconds, roughly 10 to the 3.5
About 86,400 seconds, roughly 10 to the 5
About 604,800 seconds, roughly 10 to the 5.8
About 1,000,000 seconds, roughly 10 to the 6
why: A day is 86,400 seconds, which is about 10 to the 5. Memorize it: daily count divided by 1e5 is roughly average QPS.
more: 3,600 is seconds in an hour; 604,800 is seconds in a week, both common distractors to avoid.

options: About 10 GB of raw storage before overhead
About 100 GB of raw storage before overhead
About 1 TB of raw storage before overhead
About 10 TB of raw storage before overhead
why: 500M records times 2 KB is 5e8 times 2e3 bytes, which equals 1e12 bytes, about 1 TB. Then add overhead for indexes and replication, roughly times 3 at RF=3.
more: Keep the unit math explicit: 2 KB is 2e3 bytes, so the product lands in terabytes, not gigabytes.

options: Its plaintext JSON is easier to inspect with curl and browser tools
Binary protobuf over HTTP/2 gives lower latency, multiplexing, and typed contracts
It is schema-free, so services can evolve payloads without coordination
It runs natively in every browser without any proxy layer
why: gRPC uses protobuf over HTTP/2: compact binary payloads, request multiplexing, bidirectional streaming, and codegen-enforced contracts. REST and JSON win for public, human-debuggable, browser-facing APIs.
more: gRPC actually requires a schema (the .proto) and browsers need grpc-web plus a proxy, so the schema-free and native-browser claims are reversed.

options: Return the full result set in one response and let the client trim it
Paginate, preferring cursor or keyset over large numeric offsets
Push filtering to the client so the server stays simple and stateless
Stream the whole table over a WebSocket as one long message
why: Paginate the endpoint. Cursor and keyset pagination scale better than OFFSET, which degrades on deep pages because the database still scans and discards the skipped rows.
more: Keyset pagination uses the last-seen sort key as a WHERE bound, so each page is an index seek rather than a growing offset scan.

options: It evicts the least-recently-used keys automatically by default
It rejects new writes by default; you must set an eviction policy for a cache
It begins spilling cold keys to disk and continues accepting writes
It samples and evicts random keys until memory drops below the limit
why: The default is noeviction: writes return errors once memory is full. TTL handles staleness, not memory pressure, so for a cache you set a policy like allkeys-lru.
more: allkeys-lru is approximate: Redis samples a handful of keys and evicts the oldest of the sample rather than maintaining an exact global LRU order.

options: The node transparently forwards the request to the owner and returns the result
The node replies MOVED with the right node, and the client refreshes its slot map
The node creates the key locally and serves future reads from itself
The cluster triggers an immediate rebalance to bring the slot to that node
why: Each key hashes to one of 16384 slots and clients cache the slot-to-node map. Ask the wrong node and it returns a MOVED reply rather than forwarding, so clients learn to aim at the right node.
more: Nodes gossip the full slot map so any node knows the owner, but they redirect rather than proxy; a transient ASK redirect is used only during slot migration.

options: Keys sharing a common prefix always land in the same slot by default
Use hash tags so only the substring inside braces is hashed for slot placement
Issue both operations inside one MULTI block so they colocate automatically
Pin both keys with a manual CLUSTER SETSLOT command on the client
why: Only the substring inside braces is hashed, so {user:123}:posts and {user:123}:likes share a slot and can be used together in a MULTI transaction. Cluster multi-key ops only work within one slot.
more: A shared textual prefix without braces does not guarantee colocation; the hash tag is what forces identical slot computation.

options: A single node cannot hold a large enough working set to be authoritative
Replication is asynchronous, so failover can drop the last acknowledged writes
Redis keeps everything in memory only and never persists to disk at all
Read throughput is too low for a system that must serve as source of truth
why: Async replication means the primary acks before the replica sees the write, so a promoted replica may never have the last acknowledged writes. Combined with RDB/AOF loss windows, acked writes can vanish.
more: Redis does persist (RDB snapshots and AOF), but the persistence windows plus async replication together leave gaps where durability is not guaranteed.

options: INCR is not atomic, so concurrent requests can lose increments
Calling EXPIRE every request keeps pushing the reset forward; set TTL only on the first
EXPIRE must run before INCR or the counter starts without a TTL
Each request needs its own key, so one EXPIRE cannot cover the window
why: If you EXPIRE on every call, steady traffic never gets a fresh window. Set expiry only when INCR returns 1, and run INCR and EXPIRE as one Lua script so a crash between them cannot leave a counter that never resets.
more: INCR is in fact atomic; the bug is the repeated EXPIRE resetting the window, not a race inside INCR itself.

options: Issue a plain DEL on the key as soon as the work is done
Verify the key still holds your token, then DEL, as one atomic Lua script
Never delete it and rely solely on the EX timeout to release the lock
Overwrite the key with an unlocked sentinel value using SET
why: Your lock may have expired and been re-acquired by someone else, so a blind DEL would delete their lock. Check-token-then-DEL must run atomically via Lua on Redis's single thread.
more: Relying only on expiry is safe but releases late, holding the lock for the full TTL even after work finishes; the check-and-delete frees it promptly and safely.

options: Configure a much longer TTL so the lock never expires mid-operation
Use a monotonic fencing token the storage layer checks; Redis has no built-in for this
Adopt the Redlock multi-node algorithm as the sole correctness guarantee
Add more replicas so the lock state survives a primary failure
why: Treat a Redis lock as an efficiency tool that occasionally fails. For correctness use fencing tokens or a consensus system like ZooKeeper or etcd; often a DB row lock or conditional update replaces the distributed lock entirely.
more: Longer TTLs and more replicas reduce the odds of a stale holder but do not prevent it; only a fencing check at the resource rejects the late writer deterministically.

options: Pub/Sub now persists undelivered messages so subscribers can replay them
Sharded Pub/Sub since Redis 7 routes each channel to the shard that owns its slot
Pub/Sub now opens a dedicated connection per channel to spread the load
The scaling complaint was never accurate even in older cluster versions
why: Classic cluster Pub/Sub broadcast every message to every node, adding no capacity. Sharded Pub/Sub via SPUBLISH and SSUBSCRIBE since Redis 7 hashes each channel to a slot, so capacity scales with shards.
more: Pub/Sub still does not persist messages, and connections are per node rather than per channel; only the routing model changed.

options: Client-side caching of the hottest keys with a short TTL
Key copies stored as product:123:1..10 so copies hash to different nodes
Read replicas, provided clients are configured to read from replicas
Read replicas pointed at a write-hot key to spread the write load
why: Client-side caching, key copies, and replica reads all spread read load. But replicas do nothing for a write-hot key, since every write still lands on the one primary that owns the slot.
more: Key copies trade consistency and write amplification for read spread; choose a copy at random on read to fan out across nodes.

options: A bug in XADD duplicates entries, so upgrading Redis resolves it
Redis cannot distinguish a slow worker from a dead one, so XCLAIM may re-run an entry
Streams do not persist entries, so add RDB snapshots to stop replays
Pub/Sub would avoid this, so replace the stream with Pub/Sub channels
why: Consumer groups track pending entries by idle time; when a worker looks dead another XCLAIMs the entry, but a slow worker looks identical to a dead one, so delivery is at-least-once. Make consumers idempotent.
more: Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget with no pending list, so it does not duplicate but also gives no delivery guarantee at all, which is worse for a work queue.

options: IP, which routes packets between hosts across networks
TCP, which provides reliable ordered byte streams between endpoints
HTTP, which structures request and response messages for the web
DNS, which resolves human-readable names to addresses
why: TCP, UDP, and QUIC sit at Layer 4 (Transport). IP is Layer 3 (Network), while HTTP, WebSockets, DNS, and WebRTC are Layer 7 (Application).
more: QUIC is often called Layer 4 even though it runs over UDP, because it provides the transport-level reliability and ordering TCP normally would.

options: About 6ms round trip at the speed of light in fiber
About 56ms round trip at the speed of light in fiber
About 200ms round trip at the speed of light in fiber
About 500ms round trip at the speed of light in fiber
why: Light in fiber travels at about two-thirds of c, roughly 200,000 km/s, so the 5,600 km round trip floors near 56ms before any processing, versus under 1ms to a nearby server.
more: This is a physical floor; real-world latency adds router hops, queuing, and TLS handshakes on top of the propagation delay.

options: L4 can inspect HTTP headers and route each frame by cookie value
L4 keeps one persistent TCP connection through to the server, as if directly connected
L4 terminates the client connection and opens a fresh one to the backend
L4 does more per-packet CPU work, which makes the path more reliable
why: L4 maintains persistent TCP connections, which suits long-lived WebSockets. L7 terminates connections and inspects content, the opposite behavior.
more: Because L4 does not parse HTTP, it cannot route by cookie or header; that capability belongs to L7, which is exactly why L7 must terminate the connection.

options: Retry with exponential backoff is the phrase interviewers are listening for
Backoff should add jitter so many clients do not retry in the same instant
Idempotent APIs make retries safe, often via an idempotency key
Circuit breakers should retry immediately and keep retrying until recovery
why: Retry with exponential backoff is the key phrase, jitter spreads retry storms, and idempotency keys make write retries safe. Circuit breakers do the opposite of retrying: they trip open and fail fast.
more: A tripped breaker periodically lets a single trial request through (half-open) rather than hammering the dependency continuously.

options: It fully supersedes TCP and should be the default for every connection
Treat it as a better TCP, but without TCP's broad baseline of adoption
It is a Layer 3 routing protocol that replaces IP for path selection
It is used only for peer-to-peer video via STUN and TURN servers
why: Consider QUIC a better TCP that still lacks TCP's broad adoption. Some performance-minded interviewers value QUIC and HTTP/3 knowledge, but most want your time spent elsewhere.
more: QUIC reduces handshake round trips and avoids head-of-line blocking across streams, but operational and middlebox support still trails TCP.

options: GraphQL, to let clients shape exactly the fields they fetch
REST, which covers the vast majority of use cases by default
gRPC, for its typed contracts and binary efficiency
WebSockets, for a persistent connection to the client
why: Default to REST; it covers about 90 percent of use cases. If unsure, say you will use REST APIs and move on.
more: Reach for GraphQL, gRPC, or WebSockets only when a specific requirement (flexible client queries, internal high-throughput RPC, or real-time push) justifies it.

options: GET, since repeating a read leaves server state unchanged
PUT, since replacing a resource with the same body is repeatable
DELETE, since deleting an already-deleted resource is a no-op
POST, since each call typically creates a new distinct resource
why: GET, PUT, and DELETE are idempotent. POST and PATCH are not guaranteed idempotent, which matters when networks fail and clients retry, since you do not want duplicate bookings.
more: Idempotency is about the end state after repeats, not the response code; DELETE may return 404 on the second call yet still be idempotent.

options: Over-fetching, where the response returns far more data than the client needs
The N+1 problem, where you run about 101 queries instead of 2
Under-fetching, where the client must make 100 follow-up round trips
Schema drift, where 100 stale type versions collide on read
why: This is the N+1 problem: 1 query for events plus N for each venue equals 101 instead of 2. Fix it with batching via a dataloader.
more: A dataloader coalesces the per-venue lookups within a tick into one batched query, collapsing N+1 back toward 2.

options: Path for optional filters and query parameters for required identifiers
Path when the value is required to identify the resource, query for optional filters
Always use query parameters since they are simpler to test and cache
Use path parameters only to carry POST request body fields
why: Tie it to required versus optional: use a path or nested resource when the value is required, like /events/{id}/tickets, and query parameters for optional filters like /tickets?event_id=123&section=VIP.
more: The path identifies which resource; query parameters refine, sort, or filter a collection of them.

options: Header versioning, which keeps URLs clean and content-negotiable
URL versioning like /v1/events, widely understood and quick to explain
Semantic version fields embedded in the request body payload
Skip versioning entirely and evolve the schema in place
why: URL versioning is the safer interview choice because it is widely understood and fast to explain. Breakdowns omit it only because most interviewers do not probe versioning, not because it is unimportant.
accept: Header versioning is a defensible real-world choice (cleaner URLs, content negotiation), but URL versioning is recommended for interviews because it communicates intent instantly.
more: Header versioning is legitimate in production, but it is harder to demonstrate and reason about quickly under interview time pressure.

options: A graph database like Neo4j for relationship-heavy domains
A relational database, specifically PostgreSQL, unless requirements signal otherwise
A document database like MongoDB for flexible evolving schemas
A key-value store like Redis serving as the system of record
why: Resist exotic databases; most of the time the right answer is relational, and PostgreSQL is the recommended default unless you have strong opinions otherwise.
more: Postgres also supports JSONB, full-text search, and geo extensions, so reaching for a specialized store is rarely justified at interview scope.

options: Neo4j cannot represent many-to-many relationships cleanly
Even Facebook models its social graph on MySQL, so SQL is enough for your interview
Graph databases are unable to enforce foreign-key constraints
Graph databases are slower than key-value stores at multi-hop traversals
why: Even Facebook models its social graph on MySQL, and LinkedIn and Twitter use SQL for core relationship data, so graph databases usually add needless complexity.
more: Facebook's TAO is a graph-aware caching and serving layer built on top of sharded MySQL, not a native graph database.

options: Time values are not valid partition keys for a sharded table
All current writes hit the latest-range shard, creating a hot shard
It makes querying for recent posts effectively impossible
Foreign-key relationships break across time-based shard boundaries
why: Time-range sharding sends all current writes to the latest-range shard, creating a hot shard. It works better for archival or analytics where writes spread across ranges.
more: Recent-data reads can also concentrate on that same shard, so both write and read hotspots tend to land on the newest range.

options: Data volume, how much will be stored and how fast it grows
Access patterns, which queries and writes dominate the workload
Consistency requirements, how fresh and correct reads must be
Backend language, which framework and ORM the team will use
why: Everything flows from data volume, access patterns (the most important), and consistency requirements. The backend programming language is an implementation detail, not a schema driver.
more: Access patterns dominate: model the data to make the queries you run most often cheap, even at the cost of some normalization.

options: Use business data like email so keys stay human-readable
Use system-generated IDs like user_id, which stay stable as business rules change
Always build composite primary keys spanning several columns
Omit explicit primary keys and rely on row insertion order
why: Use system-generated IDs such as user_id and post_id over business data like email, because system keys stay stable even when business rules change.
more: A business value like email can change or be reassigned, which would cascade into every foreign key referencing it.

options: A balanced B-tree ordered by the primary key
A heap file, holding rows in no particular order
A hash table bucketed by the primary key value
An LSM tree kept sorted by insertion timestamp
why: The main table is a heap file: a collection of rows in no particular order, like a notebook where you write entries as they arrive.
more: An index adds the ordered structure on top; the heap itself just appends rows wherever there is free space.

options: Frequent reads with infrequent writes, like a reporting view
Frequent writes with infrequent reads, like a logging table
Any table once it grows past roughly a million rows
A table that is only ever queried by its primary key
why: Write-heavy, read-light tables like logs are the classic case where index maintenance overhead is not justified. Tiny tables are similar, where a sequential scan beats traversing an index.
more: Every insert must also update each index, so on a write-dominated table indexes tax the hot path while rarely paying off on reads.

options: Nodes can have hundreds of children, unlike a binary tree's two
Each node is sized to fit one disk page, about 8KB in Postgres
Finding id=350 in Postgres might read only 2 to 3 pages from disk
B-trees support only equality lookups, never range scans
why: B-tree nodes have hundreds of children, fit a roughly 8KB page, and a lookup may read only 2 to 3 pages (root, maybe an internal node, then leaf). B-trees handle both equality and range, so the last statement is false.
more: The high fan-out is what keeps the tree shallow, so even large tables resolve in a handful of page reads.
